data.title: Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF: Quality Discipline For Turbulent Times
data.description:
Introduction

In uncertain times, and we are living in uncertain times, investors often pivot to dividend stocks and ETFs. Some investors think one dividend ETF is pretty much as good as another, but this is not the case, mostly due to differences in focus. These differences affect the yield, growth, concentration, sector exposure, and valuation. VIG (VIG), SCHD (SCHD), and DGRO (DGRO) all focus on dividend-paying U.S. equities, but they have different philosophies that change their returns over time.

Methodologies

The rule that defines VIG is that a holding has to have ten consecutive years of dividend growth. If a company misses one increase, they're out of the game. This filters companies for durability above all else. Yield is not the core selection criterion, nor is value necessarily. VIG excludes new entrants whose yield lands in the top 25% of eligible stocks, because high yields can potentially signal pressure on payout ratios. Existing constituents are removed if they enter the top 15% at rebalance. VIG's design specifically avoids the high-yield trap.

SCHD has a different methodology. It starts with requiring ten consecutive years of dividend payments, then ranks stocks on quality metrics including cash flow to total debt, return on equity, dividend yield, and five-year dividend growth rate. As a result, the fund is more concentrated and yields more. DGRO has other benchmarks, excluding companies whose payout ratios indicate the dividend may be getting ahead of earnings. The fact that VIG uses a single filter, ten years of uninterrupted dividend growth, is in some ways the most simple and ruthless of the three.

Yield And Dividend Growth

There are significant differences between the funds' yields. VIG currently sits at around 1.56%, DGRO at approximately 2.0%, and SCHD leads at roughly 3.3%. These differences are a direct result of the methodologies used. VIG's exclusion of the highest-yielding companies and its emphasis on reinvestment-oriented businesses naturally leads to lower current income.

In terms of growth, SCHD and VIG have both posted five-year dividend growth rates of approximately 9.1%, while DGRO has increased at around 7.1% over the same period. VIG's competitive growth rate is driven by the compounding nature of its ten-year filter. Companies that have grown dividends for a decade tend to keep doing so.

The practical implication is that an investor in SCHD collects more income today but is betting on a different set of companies. An investor in VIG accepts a lower starting yield in exchange for a portfolio made up of businesses that have already proven they can grow distributions through multiple cycles.

Size, Fees, And Concentration

VIG is the largest dividend ETF in the market by AUM at around $105 billion. SCHD has grown quickly to approximately $86 billion. DGRO is the smallest at roughly $39 billion. All three are easily large enough that liquidity is not an issue.

On fees, VIG charges 0.04%, SCHD 0.06%, and DGRO 0.08%. Over a thirty-year holding period those basis points compound, but they don't make a meaningful difference at current levels.

Concentration is considerably more important as a metric. SCHD holds around 100 companies, with its top ten representing a substantial portion of total assets. VIG holds roughly 350 companies with materially lower top-ten concentration. That is not a value judgment on SCHD, as concentration can cut both ways. But for investors who want behavior closer to the broad market and also want to impose a quality filter, VIG's breadth provides it.

Sector Exposure And What It Means In Practice

VIG is currently weighted toward technology at around 26%, financials at roughly 21%, and healthcare at about 16%. That is a less traditional dividend profile than investors might expect. The ten-year growth requirement has included mega-cap names like Broadcom (AVGO), Microsoft (MSFT), and Apple (AAPL). Not classic dividend stocks, but large-cap compounders that happen to have ten-year growth streaks.

SCHD's quality and yield screen produces a different mix, favoring financials, consumer defensive, and energy. That generates higher current yield but higher sensitivity to interest rate moves and commodity cycles. DGRO's sector profile resembles VIG's in some ways, but its payout ratio constraint removes companies distributing the majority of earnings, keeping the portfolio focused on businesses that retain capital.

These differences are most important during regime shifts. In technology-led bull markets, VIG and DGRO tend to do well. In inflationary environments or when investors rotate toward value, SCHD tends to do better.

Valuation: Paying Up For Quality

None of these funds are cheap in absolute terms. VIG's tech-heavy weighting means it has a relatively high forward P/E, reflecting investor willingness to pay for consistent cash flow growth rather than look for a valuation mismatch. SCHD often trades at a slightly lower multiple due to its yield orientation and sector mix. DGRO tends to sit between the two.

A simplified reverse DCF check is useful here. If VIG trades at roughly 25 times earnings based on current aggregate holdings, the implied long-term growth rate required to justify an 8 to 9 percent annual return works out to the mid-single digits. That aligns with historical earnings growth for large-cap dividend growers. The valuation is not high relative to its own history. It's just not cheap on an absolute basis.

The key question is not which fund has the cheapest valuation. It is what growth assumption is embedded and whether that assumption is reasonable given the underlying portfolio. For VIG, the assumption looks grounded. For SCHD, the lower multiple may reflect genuine value or lower expected growth from its yield-heavy composition.

Performance In Context

Over the five years ending in late 2025, VIG delivered a total price return of approximately 60.9% and DGRO came in close behind at 60.3%. SCHD trailed at 38.9% over the same period, reflecting the underperformance of its yield-heavy, value-oriented sector mix during a technology-led market. Over ten years, VIG and DGRO are nearly identical at 190% and 189% respectively, while SCHD delivered 146%.

VIG and DGRO have outperformed SCHD over both time frames, driven largely by their technology exposure. That may well shift when interest rates fall and higher yields become relatively more attractive. The historical record suggests these funds take turns in the lead based on market regime, but the recent gap is significant.

Conclusion

If immediate and relatively high income is the priority, SCHD is structurally designed to deliver it. The 3.3% yield versus VIG's 1.56% is not a minor difference, but the result of a deliberate methodological choice. If broad diversification and dividend growth persistence are the priority, VIG offers the cleanest implementation. It does not chase yield, does not concentrate aggressively, and enforces discipline through a dividend growth filter that has real implications. If a balance between payout sustainability and moderate growth is more important, DGRO is a solid middle ground. I rate VIG a Buy.

data.title: Palantir: My First Ever 'Buy' Following The U.S.-Iran Escalation And Anthropic Cancellation
data.description:
Palantir Overview

If you read my previous articles on Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), you probably know that I've been a big skeptic regarding the stock's prospects. First, I was skeptical about PLTR's business model. But when the firm started to actively monetize its AIP and other offerings, scaling and driving up the growth rates amid the market's rising needs for AI solutions (on both the government and commercial sides), I turned neutral. The only thing that kept me on the sidelines was Palantir's valuation - it was simply impossible to explain it by any conventional metric (even when matched with growth rates). However, Palantir went through a noticeable correction recently, falling by over 30% off its ATH at some point - all because of a multiple contraction.

The fundamentals that PLTR has shown in its most recent Q4 2025 report can't explain this dip - they were stunning, in my opinion (the CEO called the firm's performance "truly iconic", and I tend to agree). Palantir's Q4 revenue went up by 70% YoY to $1.41 billion, beating the consensus estimate by 4.88%, according to Seeking Alpha. The Rule of 40 was actually the Rule of 127 for PLTR (that 70% revenue growth stacked against a 57% adjusted operating margin), so we can call the overall business expansion a "high-quality" one.

Both commercial and government revenues soared by 137% and 66% YoY, respectively. If enterprises were more rapidly adopting AIP (hence the massive YoY growth in Q4), the DoD (the Department of Defense) was responsible for the 66% growth that came from the deep integration of Palantir's operating system and overall tech stack. PLTR's management sees even more opportunities ahead, having guided for FY2026 sales of ~$7.19 billion (+61% YoY), far exceeding the previous market's consensus of $6.2 billion. As Palantir's business model has become exceptionally capital-light thanks to the AIP rollout, the company is scaling its net income at extraordinary paces - in Q4, the NI came in at $608 million, and the cash from operations reached $773 million (the CAPEX was at $13.3 million, which is why I use the terms "capital light"). As a result, the non-GAAP Q4 EPS went up by over 78% YoY, beating the consensus by 8.60%:

So, the stock's YTD drop came primarily from the multiple contraction - the market started to reassess the longevity of Palantir's model scaling, and the risks coming from Claude (you know, the well-documented SaaS-Pocalypse).

I assume that since you're reading an article on PLTR, you probably know the above data and sell-off likely reasons. So let's switch to what's happening now - the Anthropic (ANTHRO) directive and the AI power shift that might impact PLTR and some other big techs such as Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), and Microsoft (MSFT). Pete Hegseth shared on X that the Trump administration ordered federal agencies to cease using Anthropic's tech:

In conjunction with the President's directive for the Federal Government to cease all use of Anthropic's technology, I am directing the Department of War to designate Anthropic a Supply-Chain Risk to National Security. Effective immediately, no contractor, supplier, or partner that does business with the United States military may conduct any commercial activity with Anthropic. Anthropic will continue to provide the Department of War its services for a period of no more than six months to allow for a seamless transition to a better and more patriotic service.

Source: Secretary of War Pete Hegseth, @SecWar on X

As a result, it has created a seismic shift in the AI landscape - a lot of investors started to question how this situation would affect PLTR (and other tech names).

For Amazon and Alphabet, which are Anthropic's primary financial backers, the loss of a $200 million federal contract looks like a bearish thing. But I don't think it's a major downside for future valuation upgrades and IPO plans, as most of Anthropic's projected $14 billion in 2026 revenue comes from non-government sectors.

Making that distinction clear is crucial for Anthropic because most of its projected $14 billion in revenue this year comes from businesses and government agencies that are using Claude for computer coding and other tasks. More than 500 customers are paying Anthropic at least $1 million annually for Claude, according to a announcement disclosing an investment that had valued the company at $380 billion.

The Hindu

So, for AMZN and GOOG, I don't think there's a major risk to their stakes in Anthropic - the AI startup is likely to raise a higher valuation for its IPO this year (or next year), hence boosting value for GOOG and AMZN.

But the tactical winner in today's situation is Microsoft - their existing agreements for their specialized Azure for U.S. Government Top Secret cloud might help them absorb the combat simulation workflows once held by Anthropic. A few hours after the Anthropic-Pentagon rift, OpenAI, where MSFT holds a major stake, reached an agreement with the DoD, accepting the "all lawful use" standard that Anthropic rejected:

So, effectively, in my understanding, Azure will deploy OpenAI's models (including GPT-5.2) into the heart of the military's classified networks for combat simulation with limited competition.

Now, back to Palantir. I don't think it really matters for the firm which LLM provider will be used - Palantir partners with both OpenAI and Anthropic. Their Ontology tech was designed as a model-agnostic brain that is interoperable with any model. During the Epic Fury operation, Palantir's tech was transforming disorganized data streams into intuitive, real-world objects, and I don't think it really mattered what reasoning engine was used - Claude or GPT-5.2 - all this is secondary to the COP (Common Operating Picture) created by Palantir's stack. For more information on how Palantir's tech functions in general, please refer to the recent article by Dmytro Lebid.

[Palanti Gotham] platform is meant to find cause-and-effect relationships in huge amounts of data, and it led to the creation of Project Maven (the 2017 Defense Department initiative) to speed up the integration of AI and machine learning tech into military intelligence workflows. Nowadays, it covers a bunch of apps that help with military operations in Iran, show all the data, give real-time analysis, and provide data for training machine learning models, all to make military attacks and defense more effective. For the purposes of the Department of Defense, products from Palantir Apollo are also used, where programs are provided to protect network infrastructure in the combat zone. Nevertheless, the PLTR software is also important for commercial structures that aim to protect their own networks and clouds from external interference.

Source: The recent article by Dmytro Lebid

We could have seen from the case with Anthropic that Claude, or any other LLM, isn't really a threat to Palantir's business model - at least it's not threatening the government side. The US is moving $1.5 trillion in defense spending for 2027, and I think that a) this budget is going to grow over time as geopolitical conflicts do not ease, and b) the share of AI and IT spending in general within this budget is likely to expand as the new war practices have shown the rising need for new tech. Plus, speaking about the commercial business, I think we could have seen some deterioration in Q4 data if the SaaS-Pocalypse risks were real.

For the first time in my coverage history on PLTR, I think the multiple contraction has already gone too far given the above setup.

The forward P/E and P/S ratios have dropped by ~54% and over 50% since the beginning of 2026, respectively, per YCharts data:

At the same time, the sales consensus revisions look very bullish - since the Q4 release in early February, analysts have increased their estimates dramatically (not just for FY2026, but mainly for long-dated estimates, 2029+):

If this momentum slows but continues further, we'll likely see ~$7.5 billion in sales for FY2026 and $12-13 billion in FY2027 (versus today's $7.27 billion and $10.34 billion for the subsequent years, respectively). At the higher end of my FY2027 sales assumption, PLTR's implied P/S ratio turns to ~27x. It isn't cheap, but it's not the current TTM P/S ratio of 76x+ that most people look at. Assuming $17-18 billion in FY2028 sales, and putting a multiple of 25x on it, I get an upside potential of over 24% in the new 12-24 months.

Final Words

The main risk to my thesis is that investors decide to further re-rate PLTR stock to at least 20x FY2028 sales, and my premium on the current consensus doesn't materialize. If both of these events happen at the same time, the implied "fair" market cap would drop to $298.4 billion (in the next 12-24 months). Today's market cap stands at ~$352.1 billion, according to YCharts, so the downside risk is ~15-16%. At a lower exit P/S like 10-15x, the stock might fall by 25-30% from today's levels.

Despite the risks, I decided to upgrade my rating on PLTR from Hold to Buy - it's my first ever bullish rating on Palantir stock. I like the business model's scaling, and I don't see major risks from the recent Anthropic case. The upside might come from PLTR's deeper integration into DoD's workflows, and some possible boost from the commercial side of PLTR's business. I see there's an upside potential of ~24% in the next year or two. PLTR is a "Buy" on this dip.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: General Dynamics: Inexpensive Defense Leader
data.description:
Introduction

I recently wrote an article discussing a bunch of defense stocks that saw big gains as a result of the escalation of the Middle East war. There, I talked about some of the companies I've broken down before, like Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Northrop Grumman (NOC), and I touched on some new names for me. One of those was General Dynamics Corporation (GD), and I noticed this specific stock for a couple of reasons. The first one is the fact that it stands out from this group as having the least impressive stock returns in the last year, which could potentially mean an opportunity. The second one is the current focus of this company on submarines, which, in general, is a fascinating topic to discuss, even if it's not as widely needed for the current warfare scene as fighter jets.

So today, I'll break down this stock, take a look at where the company is headed, and try to understand why it lags behind its peers and whether it's a good buying opportunity. Before I begin, I'd like to share a few thoughts I had about defense stocks and the perception of investors of the stocks that "historically" underperform the market.

How to look at the defense stocks (not only)?

Let's talk about different views on investing, sentiment, and why there are so many disputes about whether to stay long in a stock or sell and never look back. I'll show you a couple of graphs that prove there is little reason to look at the historical performance of the stock and explain why I never give my readers any actionable suggestions, even if I believe what I see is right.

This is not the first time I am breaking down a defense sector stock, and I have a large interest in the topic due to my exposure to most of the world's ongoing wars - I am from Ukraine, and all of my family is still living there, while I live in Israel. I got a combo!

As my interest in the sector has increased, one of the first companies I broke down was Lockheed Martin, for its best-in-class fighting jets. As a non-experienced investor in defense, I rated Lockheed as a Buy, breaking down its fundamental metrics, management's efforts to "clean up" to start performing better, and its absolute leadership in its plane developments. The first article got a strong interest from readers, many of them pointing out that these metrics aren't good enough, as it doesn't matter what the company does; the stock constantly underperforms the defense industry, as well as the overall market.

After reading those comments, I went to check the performance for myself and got concerned. The stock had seriously underperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) on a total return basis in the last three years (including dividends paid to investors but not reinvested). As a matter of fact, even since my initial Buy rating, the stock is up by over 60%. Looking at the performance of the last three years to date, you would have been much better off investing in the S&P.

As I said in one of the podcasts I participated in, I think of myself as a momentum investor. It doesn't mean I don't care about anything else besides the sentiment. As a matter of fact, the most favorite part for me in investing is breaking down different business models opportunities, predicting business decisions, or even suggesting ones. But at the same time, I recognize the importance of sentiment in today's market, and it can impact my decision in some cases. However, so far, this approach has proved to be incredibly successful when talking of AI, tech, and the modern, explosive, exciting part of the market but to be completely irrelevant when taking a look at the defensive, dividend plays, including the defense itself.

Even if you bought LMT exactly 3 years ago and it has considerably underperformed the S&P, it doesn't mean my call to Buy would prove to be wrong, looking at the "historical" performance. If, instead of a three-year timeframe, I chose 1 year when I was making this graph, the picture would reverse. It turns out LMT is "historically" a genius investment! It returned over 50%, while the S&P returned south of 20%.

Moreover, if you take a graph and play with it, you would see that it drastically changes over the years. Looking at 1 year, LMT wins. Three years – S&P has the lead. Five years – LMT is on top. 10 years – the market is doing better again. It might look like a very obvious thing - past performance isn't an indicator of future results, and all of that. All of us have heard it so many times, but when we come to the situation where we see our money in the red, or we realize we make much less than we could have, all this knowledge quickly leaves the chat, leaving us with anxiety and the need to write someone in the comments that they will have the same fate investing in the stock.

But what does it mean for me, as someone who follows the momentum? First, if you want to look and follow the sentiment, it is relevant only on short distances, never long. Second, the fact that someone got "burned" or profited greatly doesn't mean anything about your position. Look at the numbers, prospects, backlog, and opportunities for business, and don't follow someone's advice blindly. The fact that the stock had made over 50% in a year doesn't mean there were no investors who closed this position in major losses this very year. The fact that the industry or a company has been lagging, even for a very long time, doesn't mean it won't prove to be the best pick you've made.

Returning this conversation to General Dynamics, this same thing can be said about this stock. It had done better than the S&P in the past year, but it had underperformed it over the last three years. Also, it is lagging behind the overall defense sector by a wide margin. In this specific situation, I view this as an opportunity more than anything else. Given the company's position, the backlog, and the transition to making money, it could prove to be the best pick in defense in the foreseeable future. The past performance has little relevance here, as in the past, the company has been spending and preparing for what should come next. And now, let's finally get to the company, the results, and what I saw there, which made me think it's positioned for growth.

General Dynamics

As it's my first time covering this stock, and some of my followers may never have heard about this company before, I'll quote the company overview from the main page on Seeking Alpha:

It operates through four segments: Aerospace, Marine Systems, Combat Systems, and Technologies. The Aerospace segment produces and sells business jets; and offers aircraft maintenance and repair, management, aircraft-on-ground support, customer support and custom completion services, modifications, upgrades, and lifecycle sustainment support services. The Marine Systems segment designs and builds nuclear-powered submarines, surface combatants, and auxiliary ships for the United States Navy and Jones Act ships for commercial customers, as well as provides maintenance, modernization, and lifecycle support services for navy ships; offers and program management, planning, engineering, and design support services for submarine construction programs. The Combat Systems segment manufactures land combat solutions, such as wheeled and tracked combat vehicles, Stryker wheeled combat vehicles, piranha vehicles, weapons systems, energetics and munitions, mobile bridge systems with payloads, tactical vehicles, main battle tanks, and armored vehicles; and offers modernization programs, support and sustainment services, and development programs. The Technologies segment provides information technology solutions and mission support services; mobile communication, computers, and command-and-control mission systems; intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance solutions to military, intelligence, and federal civilian customers; cloud services, cybersecurity, network modernization, artificial intelligence; machine learning; application development, high-performance computing, and 5G and advanced communications services; and unmanned undersea vehicle manufacturing and assembly services.

Looking at the results, General Dynamics had a very productive year in 2025. It showed double-digit growth and a record-breaking backlog, meaning the company's growth will only be accelerating from here. Revenue for the year is at $52.5 billion, which is a 10% increase from the year before, and their earnings per share rose even better, by 13.4%. Even though those are very solid numbers, the best part, in my opinion, is a massive total backlog of $118 billion, with an impressive 30% increase. It shows that demand for their products (both for jets and submarines) is at its best levels it has been for the past decade or so. While the company faced some headwinds like supply chain difficulties and was affected by tariffs, it can get about $4 billion in free cash flow from those revenues, which is clearly a good sign. This allows them to invest in their factories, making sure they continue providing value, and by this, keep the demand growing.

The Aerospace segment's revenue went up 17% to $13.1 billion, which means it's one of the main drivers of the growth. It was largely due to the delivery of 158 aircraft, including the G700 and G800 models. Even with higher production, the demand remained so strong that for every plane they delivered, they took orders for more than one new one:

Importantly, for the year, Aerospace revenue of $13.1 billion is 16.5% greater than 2024. This is on top of a 30.5% growth in 2024 over 2023. Revenue growth was driven in large part by the delivery of 158 new aircraft, which is 22 more than a year ago. Earnings of $1.75 billion are up 19.3% over 2024.

So let's talk a little about demand. It was a strong quarter bordering on exceptional. Aerospace had a book-to-bill of 1.3x in the quarter and Gulfstream alone had an aircraft book-to-bill of 1.4x, even as deliveries increased significantly in the quarter.

Orders exceeded our internal plan. The delivery of the G700 and G800 and their performance in customer hands is driving increased demand for them, which we experienced in the quarter. We continue to see improved interest across all models in all sales jurisdictions. Interestingly, the overall number of prospects in all areas continues to increase.

This "book-to-bill" success was almost the same in the Combat Systems group, which saw a surge in international interest, particularly from European customers looking for munitions and tactical vehicles. In fact, their backlog grew by a staggering 60% as international demand reached what the leadership described as "explosive" levels.

About the Marine Systems division, the situation is slightly more nuanced. The company is right now going through a transition in this segment. They are switching from investing heavily in it to a phase of faster production and delivery. For a few years, the company has been paying a lot of money for the shipyards, like Electric Boat, in order to prepare for elevated demand in the Navy. These investments have funded facility upgrades to handle the simultaneous construction of Virginia-class attack submarines and Columbia-class ballistic missile submarines. Now, the company is finally seeing the first signs that these investments are paying off.

In 2025, Electric Boat achieved a 13% increase in submarine tonnage produced. As a result, operating earnings increased 72.5% for the Marine Systems group in the fourth quarter of 2025, with margins rising by 210 basis points as construction costs stabilized. Looking forward to this year, even with a projected 79% increase in capital expenditures to over $900 million, the company expects to reach a 100% free cash flow conversion rate. The profit from these submarine investments is expected to be especially noticeable as the company increases its revenue to between $17.3 billion and $17.7 billion while expanding margins, eventually turning its $52.3 billion Marine backlog into a predictable source of income. At the same time, even though the backlog is up significantly from the $39.8 billion reported at the end of 2024, it is flat and has even declined quarter over quarter.

If we are accounting for the massive work they do to complete the orders, it's actually great that the backlog is not falling hard behind, but I would still like to be sure the demand will still be there beyond these $50 billion, so I'll be following this metric in the future.

The Technologies segment managed to grow its revenue and backlog, even with all the problems with government spending, etc. Even with external pressures, the segment ended the year with a record total estimated contract value of nearly $50 billion, which is great.

Valuation and Risks

General Dynamics had a great year in 2025, but now they have to prove they can actually build everything they’ve promised. The biggest problem is still the supply chain, with bottlenecks being some of the part suppliers, which is slowing down production. Any problem with the production can send the stock down, as it would mean the company will not be able to deliver its ships and planes on time. On top of that, tariffs cost them $41 million in 2025 and are expected to be an even bigger problem in 2026. There is also a financial risk regarding the company's debt. They have $1 billion to pay back soon, and because interest rates have gone up since they took the loan, it’s going to cost them more.

Valuation doesn't scream the stock is expensive, but it is also nuanced. The stock looks cheap on a P/E basis but appears expensive when you look at the PEG ratio. The forward GAAP P/E of 22.41 is about 3.3% lower than the sector median of 23.18, so you're paying a slight discount for every dollar of projected profit compared to other companies. At the same time, the PEG ratio (price to earnings divided by growth) is at 2.52, which is significantly higher than the sector average of 1.85. It means that while the stock price is fair relative to current earnings, it is forecast to grow at a slower pace compared to the broader aerospace and defense industry’s projected average growth (about 9% to 20%).

Conclusion

General Dynamics is not a screaming Buy, but at the same time, it has potential. I like its focus on submarines and the ability to diversify into different segments of the defense industry. I like the backlog, the growth, and the outlook is fine as well. I see the risks, but in my opinion, taking into account the valuation, you can get this stock at a reasonable price, while many of the peers have already surged far beyond those levels. Is it a good enough reason to buy a stock? Depends on who you are asking. For an investor looking for a value opportunity, this isn't it. For those who look for explosive growth, most likely, GD won't make it (no one can be sure, but it's not expected to happen). For an investor who is into defense or is looking for exposure there, it could be attractive. Especially if you consider the 1.6% dividend, with 31 years of growth, it could prove to be a good pick.

If you want some exposure without risking it too much, it could be a nice idea to compromise on an ETF that holds General Dynamics. For wider exposure, I can suggest taking a look at First Trust Indxx Aerospace & Defense ETF (MISL), which has almost 8% in GD, or at Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD), which has a 7% exposure there. There is also the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA), which has less than 4%, and even a cybersecurity ETF like Amplify Cybersecurity ETF (HACK), which holds 6% in this stock.

data.title: AutoNation: Shifting Gears Toward Higher-Margin Growth
data.description:
Introduction

AutoNation (AN) reported its Q4'25 results last month the business has been improving from a cyclical vehicle seller into a more stable, higher-margin operator fueled by recurring revenue streams. Although top-line trends show a bit of softer demand especially on volumes, profitability held up better than many expected which is being driven by continued momentum in aftersales and customer financing. I see this as evidence that the company’s earnings mix is becoming more durable and less dependent on pure unit volume. With the stock trading at a valuation that remains undemanding relative to its cash flow profile and long-term growth potential, I believe the market is still underappreciating the improvements underway.

AutoNation's Q4 Results

AutoNation closed out the year with a mixed Q4 where a 4% decline in total revenue to $6.9 billion (which missed by $282 million) was offset by a beat on the bottom line with EPS of $5.08, 20 cents above consensus estimates. While vehicle sales were soft due to tough comps and lower volume, the company had record gross profits in Aftersales and Customer Financial Services (CFS) which helped maintain decent profitability.

To put this into context, even with a drop year over year in Q4, FY’25 revenue was still up 3% compared to the prior year at $27.6 billion with free cash flow generation of over $1 billion that helped fund a massive capital return strategy including the repurchase of 10% of the company's outstanding shares. In terms of the fundamentals, CFS was up 8% with aftersales up 5%. With the company’s lending arm, AutoNation Finance, originations have effectively doubled to over $2.2 billion.

Overall, I view AutoNation’s financing arm as a ‘hidden gem’ within the business since it allows the company to capture high-margin interest income that previously used to go to third-party banks. Now, it’s AutoNation that has the system where they own the entire customer lifecycle from initial loan to eventual trade-in.

As for the company’s profitability this quarter, even with revenue headwinds, the company did have gross profit in the quarter only 2% lower at $1.2 billion. While new and used unit volumes faced macro headwinds, a 6% increase in service-related gross profit acted as a critical hedge, preserving the bottom line. This shift in the revenue mix allowed the company to maintain stability even as the traditional "metal-moving" side of the business softened.

The primary driver of this resilience was a record $2,891 gross profit per unit in the finance office with a 75% finance penetration rate. In my view, this is an impressive metric since it means that a one-time car sale essentially becomes a predictable, multi-year stream of cashflows. Historically, car sales (both new and used) tend to be quite cyclical and collecting interest and service from customers over a 5-7 year period creates a business profile that’s more akin to a higher margin banking business rather than a low-margin car lot.

In terms of the aftersales margins, AutoNation say margins expand by 80bps to 48.7% thanks to improved technician retention and a 3% same-store increase in franchise technician headcount, and better labor productivity. Like the financing side of the business, the parts and services business is also one of high margins and it accounts for nearly half of AutoNation’s total gross profit, despite representing a much smaller fraction of overall company revenue.

As for the balance sheet, AutoNation has modest leverage at 2.4x, roughly unchanged from last year and well within the target range of 2-3x. With an investment grade credit rating of Baa3 from Moody’s and a BBB- rating from S&P, both with a stable outlook, the company has access to a low cost of capital. That gives it flexibility when it comes to making capital allocation decisions.

On capital allocation, the company’s free cash flow of $1.1 billion enabled significant return of capital to shareholders in FY’25. This was evenly split between reinvestment in the business ($309 million in maintenance capex and $460 million in M&A) and shareholder returns. A total of $785 million used for share repurchases that reduced shares outstanding by 10% at an average price of $193. As I’ll discuss in the valuation section, I think share repurchase make a lot of sense at these levels given the dislocation between what the credit markets and equity market think of this company.

Set up well for 2026

I’m bullish on AutoNation’s longer-term prospects and its earnings potential in 2026. Management is cautious on the macro backdrop with guidance for new vehicle volumes to be down 2-5% with the drivers of this being affordability challenges and consumer sensitivity to monthly payments. On the conference call post quarter, management did call out some supportive factors like tailwinds from tax changes, refunds, and bonus depreciation which could offer some relief. Still, I think their tone reflects realism about a market that’s still grappling with high transaction prices and the fading impact of earlier incentive-driven demand. In the short term, especially in Q1 and Q2, new unit profitability is expected to hold steady with late 2025 levels. That tells me that AutoNation’s management intends to stay disciplined on pricing and margins rather than chase volume.

Longer term as lease returns increase, CEO Mike Manley made a good point I agree with: dealers stand to benefit from those vehicles coming back into the market and should be able to work with manufacturers on fair pricing. To me, that signals optimism for a healthier flow of used inventory without too much pressure on residual values.

What I find encouraging is the push to build momentum in recurring, higher-margin areas. As I’ve discussed, Customer financial services should continue delivering strong product attachments and financing penetration. AutoNation Finance also looks well positioned to scale profitably as its portfolio matures. Even though it’s still small with just a $6 million profit in Q4, management notes this is a “decent starting point” for 2026 and I think the financing side of the business will likely improve with the benefit of time and scale. Finally, aftersales is expected to post mid-single-digit gross profit growth, supported by technician hiring, retention, and productivity gains. That’s helpful in the context of sustaining recurring revenue even if margins get a little pressure from the growing wholesale business.

Risks to consider

One of the risks I see for the business is the potential for more sustained affordability pressures as it relates to the health of the consumer. As higher financing costs and elevated vehicle prices continue to constrain consumer demand, it’s possible that a sharper‑than‑expected pullback in new vehicle volumes or more aggressive discounting by OEMs could compress margins despite disciplined inventory management.

There’s also a second risk from the pace of electrification as OEMs recalibrate incentive structures and consumers hesitate on EV adoption. Here, the potential for reduced throughput and weaker profitability on electrified models until pricing and residual values stabilize is also a risk.

AutoNation is attractively valued

Ultimately, for what you're paying today, investors are getting compensated for said macro risks through a low valuation. While the company's valuation has rerated from 5.1x EV/EBITDA in 2022 to 9.2x EV/EBITDA today, I think this is warranted given the future growth and margin expansion.

As for that future growth, the sellside expects 7% EPS growth in 2026 followed by low-teens CAGR going forward. This puts the P/E at about 9.0x and 5.6x on 2030 numbers. Assuming AutoNation can hit these numbers, investors can expect to earn about a 14% CAGR through the end of the decade without multiple expansion. I think this should be achievable since AutoNation just bought back 10% of its share count this year which should increase EPS just via buybacks alone if they can keep it up.

When looking at analyst estimates for AutoNation, most analysts have buy ratings on the name with 8 'buy' ratings and only 5 'hold' ratings. From the current price to the average price target of $243.75 one year out, analysts see about 26.3% upside (source: Bloomberg).

Finally, when comparing AutoNation to its peers, the company trades inexpensively at 8.7x forward earnings versus 10.7x forward for the peer group (source: Bloomberg). Even on an EV/EBITDA basis, the company trades at a 0.2 turn discount to competitors. Given the higher growth at AutoNation and modest leverage, I think AutoNation is worth buying on a relative value basis.

Bottom line

While near-term vehicle demand volumes might be suboptimal this year, I think we can still expect good performance from AutoNation given that the company is expanding its financing and aftersales businesses that are steadily reshaping it into a higher-margin, more recurring revenue model. With strong free cash flow, significant share repurchases, low leverage, and an attractive valuation relative to both its growth outlook and peers, I see an opportunity for double-digit annualized returns going forward. So for investors willing to look past cyclical and short term volume issues, I think AutoNation looks like a buy.

data.title: Iran War Aftermath: The U.S. Defense Stock Boom Isn't Coming
data.description:
The war in Iran has brought aerospace and defense back into focus. At least, more in focus because aerospace and defense has been in focus ever since the start of the war in Ukraine, followed by conflict in Gaza, fights between Pakistan and India, the NATO Greenland crisis, tensions between China and neighboring countries, and President Donald Trump floating the idea of a $1.5 trillion budget which by itself does not make sense for some, but starts making a lot more sense in a multi-year war setting with a significant need for the US military to increase its capabilities.

The military action in Iran affects the broader markets in a negative as well as positive way, and in this report I will dive a bit deeper into the prospects for the US defense industry. This piece was already planned before the war commenced, but with recent military action, it becomes even more of a focal point.

Note: War often comes with an unmeasurable amount of human tragedy and a fair bit of geopolitics. While I'm cognizant of these factors, this report does not intend to discuss either the human tragedy or the politics behind the most recent combat action but aims to provide an analysis on the prospects for US defense companies.

The Somewhat Unpopular Opinion on the Effects for the U.S. Defense Industry

At the time of writing, the Aerospace ETF XAR is up 2.6% while the Aerospace ETF ITA is up 2.8%. I would say that the industry as a whole is up, but it's not necessarily a very strong increase in the valuation. That also makes some sense. First, we note that the war causes an initial positive or negative shock across asset classes and sectors. Much of that is sentiment driven and the same holds for aerospace and defense stocks. The reality is that demand for defense equipment already outpaces the ability to supply by a wide margin. So, defense contractors are not really in need of additional orders. They're in need of additional capacity to fulfill demand. The war is not going to magically create supply and demand additions to accelerate growth in future years, is expected to be conditional and largely incremental.

There Are Opportunities, Not Huge But Still Meaningful

Where we do see some incremental opportunities is in missile defense and intelligence. If there's one thing that has become clear for neighboring countries such as the UAE, Qatar, and Bahrain, it's that while they do have missile defense systems in place, they have been largely insufficient. Especially for the UAE and Qatar, often considered an oasis of peace in a chaotic region, attracting tourism and business and being able to protect visitors and citizens is important. With strikes on the airports in the region, being able to better protect assets and lives will drive some incremental demand. We're basically looking at missile defense and counter-drone solutions as candidates for incremental growth. Both of these growth areas were already in our playbook before the war in Iran, and both of these growth areas are currently more supply and development constrained than supply constrained. So, it's unlikely that it is going to be a big near-term driver of growth.

For emerging companies, it does, however, create a better entry to sell, for instance, non-kinetic counter-drone solutions, which are fit for deployment in urban areas. Two Australian companies come to mind as beneficiaries. One is Electro Optic Systems (EOPSF), and the other is DroneShield (DRSHF). Both stocks gained in the aftermath of the war, gaining 13% and 9.4%. In the US, I do believe that AeroVironment (AVAV), with its counter-drone solution, is positioned well. Ironically, the stock is down 17.4% amidst concerns about the continuation of one of its major programs.

How big or long-lived opportunities will be is going to depend on how the war evolves, and I believe a comment from Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth that this will not be an endless war dampened sentiment. The longer the war goes on, the more missiles get used, which is the second incremental opportunity. As long as there are no boots on soil, there's no ground equipment or supporting helicopter equipment at risk, or equipment that needs to be maintained, which would create opportunities for the OEMs as well as providers of logistics and base maintenance such as V2X (VVX), which I covered earlier this year. The moment there is any sign of a ground war, I believe things will dramatically change in terms of prospects. When there are boots on the ground, there's an increased risk to lives, and that will be supported by ground equipment as well as naval air support. Hegseth also seemed to signal that there's no intention of this being a regime change war. If you look at the targets in Iran, I do believe that if not the primary target, a regime change is definitely a secondary target.

Not pursuing a regime change would in the near term be somewhat of a damper for defense stocks. However, in the longer term, it may inflict substantial regional instability, where, for instance, Iran targeting neighboring countries on a daily basis will require daily missile defense and counter-drone solution deployment. So, that's again an incremental opportunity.

We also note that the responses from Iran have been nothing close to the total war they have threatened, and without any strong response from Iran, a total escalation is far away. If there's such a thing as a ladder of escalation, I would say we're still at the very bottom of it. Iran has targeted an airbase in Cyprus used by the UK, but overall I do see the conflict geographically contained and more measured than I initially expected.

That also means the opportunity for aerospace and defense equipment is not changing dramatically, while opportunities in the near term are already capped by supply chain constraints.

My Data-Driven View on the US Defense Industry Explained

At The Aerospace Forum, I like to keep my analysis driven by data more than by sentiment, and that has allowed me to assess upside and downside for industries as well as individual stocks. While a fact- and data-driven method has my preference, it's also the case that we cannot completely eliminate sentiment, as investor sentiment largely determines trading multiples. There are very few companies in this world that trade in line with their fair value constantly. Additionally, there are external factors that do affect financial performance that are hard to filter out. So, before I kick off the quantitative parts of the analysis, I will first discuss the analysis methodology.

What I did was collect revenue, EBIT, and backlog data for more than 100 companies listed by Seeking Alpha as aerospace and defense companies. Each of those companies was given a subcategory and a sub-subcategory label.

Since we're focusing on US companies, I have selected the United States in my dashboard. The companies are classified as seen in the figure above with the following subcategories used:

  1. Engines & Propulsion
  2. Defense Prime Integrators
  3. Aircraft OEMs & Prime Integrators
  4. Aerospace Structures & Components
  5. Defense Electronics & Mission Systems
  6. Defense Technology / Emerging Systems
  7. Space Systems & Launch
  8. Industrial / Nuclear Defense
  9. Naval Shipbuilding
  10. Missiles, UAS & Tactical Systems (Including Missile Defense and Counter-Drone Solutions)
  11. Aerospace Services / MRO
  12. Autonomous Systems & AAM

This already brings a first challenge. The US aerospace and defense industry is big. Many companies derive revenues from commercial as well as defense activities, and many companies are engaged in various businesses. That means that we cannot select a single category and say, "This represents the US defense business." There's simply too much cross-market activity for that. However, I highlighted Defense Prime Integrators, Defense Electronics & Mission Systems, Defense Technology, Naval Shipbuilding, and Missiles and UAS & Tactical Systems. Those have a high proportion of defense exposure. So, throughout the analysis, I will at times comment on sales, EBIT, and revenues for that subset.

We also do note that some companies, such as General Electric/GE Aerospace (GE), went through significant changes, transforming from a conglomerate to a pure-play aerospace company. So, backlog or revenue declines we see for General Electric aren’t reflective of the market environment but of the change of market focus of the company. So, at times I will also exclude GE Aerospace. Similarly, Boeing went through a crisis, and that had a substantial impact on the overall figures for aerospace and defense.

We also note that besides the Boeing (BA) crisis, the pandemic had a substantial impact on the aerospace and defense industries, which more often than not was a reflection of an inability to deliver and not so much a demand constraint. For that reason, I will assess developments from 2016-2025, 2020-2025 (pandemic bottom), 2022-2025 (easing of constraints but with an impact from inflation on labor and material costs), and year-on-year.

I have also provided adjusted figures. These are not the typical adjusted numbers to account for non-recurring items. In this case the adjustments are made to get a like-for-like comparison. Some companies had no backlog or revenues/EBIT in the base year and therefore are excluded through an adjustment. Other companies have yet to report full-year results, and that also drives a retrospective removal. Both figures, meaning the adjusted and unadjusted figures, tell a numerical story. We also note that many companies actually do not consistently provide backlog figures, and some don’t do it at all. While backlogs are one of my favorite metrics, in an industry context, they do not provide a full view of forward revenues. Some companies have extremely long-tail backlogs; some backlogs are short-cycle, converting to revenue nearly instantly. So, it is not a number that tells us everything.

Revenue Growth Is Accelerated and Is Not Just Limited to Emerging Growth

If we include all US companies, we see that revenues grew by 2.1% annually from 2016 through 2025 to a total of $519.5 billion and by 1.8% on an adjusted basis to $503.9 billion. As Boeing went through its own crisis and General Electric transformed its business, defense prime integrators saw shares in revenues climb from 37.1% to 51.2%. Excluding Boeing and GE, the revenue CAGR is 6.1%, with revenues hitting $387.7 billion and 5.7% on an adjusted basis. The core group accounted for 77.8% of the revenues in 2016 and for 80.4% of the revenues in 2025. On an adjusted basis, the defense technology companies had the highest growth rate at 28.7%, followed by 12.7% for Defense Electronics, while Missiles, UAS and Tactical Systems had a growth rate of 12.8%. We currently see that MRO names are experiencing significant growth, but from 2016 to 2025 this area grew 11.5% which is strong but substantially below some of the other growth areas.

Measured from 2020, the revenue growth was 6.5% annually, with the highest growth in Space Systems & Launch solutions at 63.7% growth, followed by 32.5% growth in emerging defense technologies 15.1% growth for Missiles, UAS and Tactical Systems. MRO grew 15.9% on an adjusted basis and 25.2% for the companies that existed and reported 2025 figures. So, this confirms the backloaded multi-year growth of launch companies and emerging companies, the critical positioning of defense electronics, and commercial MRO growth. Defense prime integrators grew revenues by 5.6%, while defense electronics providers grew by 5%.

From 2022 onwards, defense technology grew again by 33%, followed by 36.1% growth for space system launch providers, 18% growth in missiles and drones, while MRO grew 14.6%. Defense electronics grew 9.6% as the war in Ukraine set off a significant acceleration in modernization efforts in which modern architecture drives demand for chips. The new growth area was industrial and nuclear defense, mostly nuclear defense, as high-energy prices would spark demand for nuclear solutions, and power demand from data centers also is sparking demand for nuclear solutions, which often are embedded in aerospace and defense companies with exposure to nuclear and energy markets.

Year-on-year, the growth rate was 7.2% and 8.3% on an adjusted basis, and it shows that the industry as a whole is growing at a faster rate. This is driven by demand increasing and supply chain bottlenecks easing. Emerging defense once again leads the pack with 47% growth, followed by 26.6% growth in space, 18% growth in industrial and nuclear defense, 17.9% growth in missiles and drones, while defense electronics grew 4.8%, with some growth being held back due to the availability of strategic rare earth minerals critical for sensor manufacturing. Defense prime integrators grew 7.3% and by 6.1% when excluding RTX (RTX), which derives growth from commercial markets as well as defense markets. Naval shipbuilders grew sales by 8.2%.

If there's one thing that we see in the war in Iran and the intervention in Venezuela it is that while Hegseth previously seemed to imply that aircraft carriers were obsolete and many people were getting the impression that drones would be replacing fighter jets, the reality is that naval shipbuilding sales are growing and if we look at the current battlefield in Iran we see some reconnaissance drones deployed by the US and a Turkish Bayraktar drone, but we mostly are seeing fighter jets, reconnaissance and refuelling airplanes in action. The battlefield, apart from the cheap drones of Iran with limited impact, is still primarily governed by advanced fighter jets deploying missiles.

EBIT Growth Has Been Modest for Aerospace and Defense Names

While revenues grew 5.7%, EBIT growth on adjusted was just 5.5%. Quite often defense contractors are viewed as the companies lining their own pockets. However, it completely ignores the reality that many defense contractors have been burned on fixed-cost contracts, and increased global tension has also resulted in higher self-funded R&D spending. Defense prime integrators grew EBIT 4% since 2016 on 5.5% revenue growth. For naval shipbuilders, the revenue growth did not translate at all to higher EBIT, as the CAGR was in a slight downward trend. Commercial market-exposed companies did much better with 11.2% and 13.7% growth, just like defense electronics with 10% growth and missiles and drones with 28.1%. Emerging defense companies failed to show consistent growth. So, from a long-term earnings growth perspective, the industry as a whole hasn’t fared well. While EBIT for the core group grew from $22 billion to over $32 billion, the growth was realized with margin pressure, and it marked mid-single digit growth annually. The long-term trend underlines stable growth for defense companies, but the bigger growth is booked in the commercial markets.

From 2020 to 2025, EBIT grew 11.4%, and that was primarily driven by high commercial aftermarket demand and commercial airplane production reviving. This growth rate still excludes Boeing but includes the downstream value creation during that time in the supply chain and in the aftermarket business. Naval shipbuilding revenues continued to slide with a 2% negative CAGR, while defense prime integrators increased EBIT by 8.1%, driven by a revival in supply capability combined with a demand uptick, while defense electronics growth was 7.2% and missile and counterdrone solutions growth was 6.8%.

If we look at 2022-2025, we note that EBIT growth did not accelerate. It slowed down to 8.3%. Naval shipbuilding kept CAGR near zero, while defense prime integrators EBIT growth tapered, driven by the impacts of higher inflation. Defense electronics growth was 7.2%, while missile and missile defense and drone and counterdrone providers went from a loss-making position to a profitable position. The big winner was commercial, with 21.7% EBIT growth.

Year-on-year, EBIT grew 14.2%. Again, commercial leads the pack with 29.5% in MRO growth, 20.3% growth in aerostructure, and aircraft OEM growth of 10%. As naval and defense primes substantially derisked in the years prior, creating a favorable comp, the growth for those subcategories was 20.5% and 9.1%. Despite concerns on rare-earth availability, the defense electronics and mission systems businesses grew earnings at a 12.5% rate. Nuclear defense grew 4%. Emerging defense technologies grew 269.1%, driven by Palantir (PLTR). Missile defense and tactical systems actually saw earnings-driven decline, and it shows that the names such as Kratos (KTOS) and AeroVironment are very promising, but they're in the scaling phase, which often means there is no strong growth in earnings. For these companies, their prospects are bright, but they're not living up to the growth expectations yet.

With the revenues and EBIT discussed, this obviously aligns us to look at operating margins. Interestingly, the cohort from 2016 showed nothing more than margin stability, and we note that margins by subcategory ranged from 1.4% to 16.3% in 2016, and they're now mostly concentrated in the 4.5%-10% region. Margins really didn’t fare well for most categories. Defense primes saw margins decline from 12.3% to 10.7%. Naval shipbuilding margins declined from 12.1% to 6.4%. Missile and drone solutions saw margins improve from 1.4% to 4.5%. Defense technology grew margins from 11.4% to 19%. Nuclear defense margins declined from 16.3% to 10.9%, while defense electronics margins declined from 12.6% to 10.6%. The core group saw a margin decline of 1.8 points to 10.5%.

From 2020 to 2025, core group margins improved from 9.6% to 10.3%, driven by defense technology margin growth. From 2022 to 2025, margins of the eligible companies fell from 11.7% to 10.3% as defense prime integrator and naval shipbuilding margins dropped from 12.3% to 10.7%. Year-on-year, margins for those two groups increased from 10.5% to 10.7%, with core group margins improving 50 basis points to 10.8%. So, what used to be a 12% margin years ago is now a 10%-11% margin.

So driven by increased investments, new market entrants, and risk sharing with customers, margins have eroded by almost two points. Perhaps that also shows us that we're hitting a limit on how much risk the Pentagon can transfer to the supply chain through fixed-price contracts because the defense industry has already absorbed a significant margin hit. If new capabilities are desired, the defense budgets need to increase.

Growth Is Somewhat Limited From a Backlog Point of View

Backlog has grown from $278.2 billion in 2016 to $801.5 billion in 2025. That's a strong growth of 12.5% annually, but it does not necessarily provide major revenue upside due to long-term nature of the backlog. To assess growth opportunities, we look at the full cohort, meaning we just look at the 2025 backlog and 2025 revenues (but still exclude GE and Boeing as done throughout this entire analysis). Revenues were $387.7 billion with a backlog of $872.7 billion. The rule of thumb is that a comfortable backlog is 2.5x revenues. This implies $348.8 billion. This would imply no upside to sales.

The core group has $815 billion in backlog, meaning there is support for $326 billion driving 3.5% upside to sales. I believe the backlog of Palantir should also be removed, as the company has a substantially different cycle nature. This gives an $803.9 billion backlog, enabling $321.6 billion, marking a 3.4% sales upside. So, what is currently locked in the backlog is 3.4% sales upside and is below the mid-single digit growth of the core group. To unlock a 100 basis point increase in supported sales upside, the gross addition to the backlog needs to be 1.4%.

So, the growth outlook supported by the backlog is not extremely strong. To get to a 5.5% growth rate, currently there is around $22.9 billion missing from the backlog. So, there's no strong support for higher revenues at the moment, but $22.9 billion in the defense industry is also not a huge number. What this basically means is that as countries already were significantly stepping up investments in defense equipment, there's a foundation to expect continued 5.5% growth.

Middle East Customers Could Drive Growth Back to Historical Averages

The big question is whether growth could come from the Middle East. I believe that it could indeed be the case. We note that backlog additions in recent years have been $0.6-$1 billion per year, which I believe largely reflects the impact from the Biden administration and typical backlog cycle times driving timing of new orders. Before that, the average was a minimum of around $8 billion and averaged $10.6 billion. So, that's a $7.5 billion to $10 billion difference annually that has not been placed over the past few years. Order revival would drive $3 billion to $4 billion in sales, providing 100 to 130 basis points from sales growth potential, which is currently lacking. That also brings the prospective sales growth to 4.4%-4.7%, and at this point you could argue that the countries in the Middle East need to ramp up by at least $15 billion, as was done in 2020, driving 6 billion in supported revenue growth and bringing the prospective sales growth prospect to 5.3%. So what could be happening if the supply chain can handle it is not a growth above historical growth averages but instead orders from the Middle East bringing the growth back in line with historical growth rates of sales. That’s an important nuance. The incremental opportunity is not layered on top of the historical growth rates; it enables US defense primes to get their growth prospects back to the historical growth rates. The real growth has to come from somewhere else: Europe. It also underlines why it is of utmost importance to keep good relations with Europe. It's important for the growth prospects for the US defense industrial complex. It's where Trump may have created a problem for the industry by lashing out at European allies over budgets and trade, driving a push for European defense autonomy.

The military action in Iran actually also underlines the importance of Europe, as airport infrastructure in Bulgaria and Crete, along with European airspace, was used to facilitate the buildup of military presence around Iran.

Conclusion: Incremental Growth Opportunities For Aerospace And Defense But Nothing Major

I believe that in Iran, we're not seeing full-blown escalation or anything that exceeds what we could have expected. In some sense, I consider the situation in Iran and the region somewhat contained and reserved. So, there are incremental opportunities from countries in the region now seeing the need that their existing missile defense and counter-drone defense are not adequate, and that drives some sales opportunities next to replenishing missile stockpiles and replacement of downed fighter jets. We would see significantly more growth opportunities if the war is going to take longer, with a further increase in the prospects for defense companies if some sort of ground war occurs.

Over the longer term, we have seen that margins of defense core companies have been under pressure, and the backlog as an indicator of support for growth is trailing a bit when measuring it against the historical growth rates. If the supply chain and production capacity permit, I do believe that we will see a growth opportunity with roughly 200 basis points of sales support coming from the Middle East. Everything that they order above $15 billion annually would drive additional growth, with the main growth opportunity still being in Europe. Important to note is that we're not talking about increments of mid or high single digits. Instead, we're talking about low single-digit additions to the growth prospects to bring the growth more in line with what we have seen over the longer term, and from there, other regions or higher spending from the US are going to drive the upside. So, I remain bullish on the aerospace and defense industry, as they have just recently turned the page on de-risking activity that affected earnings, and they are seeing missile and missile defense production capacity coming online with incremental opportunities ahead.

So, the overall conclusion is that I don't expect major double-digit or even high single-digit growth prospects, but we're seeing incremental opportunities to drive a growth acceleration toward historical growth rates and beyond, driven by order inflow from various geographical areas that do not include the Middle East, but also Europe and the Asia-Pacific. In some way the indirect impact of the Iran conflict would drive bigger growth opportunities in other geographies, as we have been seeing in recent years that one escalation quite often is followed by conflicts in other geographical areas.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CoreWeave Looks Broken... The Backlog Says Otherwise
data.description:
On Feb. 26, 2026, CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) reported its fourth-quarter and FY2025 earnings. In Q4, EPS (diluted) was -$0.56, missing consensus estimates by 13.48%. For FY2025, EPS (diluted) was -$1.39, which also fell short of the consensus by 5.77%.

What concerns me is the fact that CoreWeave is expanding through debt. The result is exploding interest expenses, which could become an existential risk if not executed properly. Indeed, some investors might agree, and the stock dropped about 25% since earnings.

Of course, there are reasons to be optimistic about the company’s potential as well, specifically, the backlog, which suggests ARR could triple in the near future- something I don’t believe the market has fully priced in.

Spoiler: With everything outlined in this article, my Buy rating for CoreWeave stays in place.

What happened in the fourth quarter and full-year 2025?

Revenue for the quarter rose 110% to around $1.57 billion. For the full year, revenue rose 168% to around $5.13 billion compared to the same period last year. CEO Mike Intrator even said it’s the fastest cloud company to have ever reached such levels:

CoreWeave is the fastest cloud in history to reach $5 billion in annual revenue. We remain in the early stages of the most transformative infrastructure build-out in history, and CoreWeave is at the forefront, building and operating some of the largest purpose-built AI clusters for the world's most demanding workloads.

When we consider the historical growth curves of the top three cloud providers, that claim isn’t so far-fetched. But of course, growth like that doesn’t come cheap. In fact, CoreWeave’s operating income flipped to a $89 million loss in the fourth quarter (from $113 million profit a year ago), thanks to operating expenses surging roughly 2.6x to around $1.66 billion in the quarter alone. As a result, full-year operating figures were also weighed down by a $46 million loss from a $324 million gain in 2025.

A large chunk of this operating expense was technology and infrastructure costs, which reached $950 million during the quarter, while over $2.9 billion was spent for the entire year. As a result, CoreWeave’s net loss for the quarter surged almost 800% to $452 million.

Now, I think it’s important to note that CoreWeave’s performance on the bottom line isn’t because of poor cost management. It's undertaking one of the most aggressive infrastructure buildouts in the AI cloud space right now, and I think this cost is the direct tradeoff.

For long-term investors, the losses are just headlines at this point, since companies building at this speed almost never optimize for profitability during the deployment phase. I think the real question comes down to how quickly deployed capacity starts converting into actual revenue, and at what margin.

On a non-GAAP basis, adjusted EBITDA came in at $898 million in the fourth quarter at a 57% margin, and around $3 billion for the entire year with 60% margin. Margin measures how much cash the core business generated before costs related to debt and equipment wear-and-tear. Those margins are still healthy despite some compression from mid-60% levels from a year ago.

Meanwhile, adjusted operating income for the quarter declined to $88 million from $121 million in the same period last year, which means the margin also compressed- from 16% to 6%. Now, I think this is something that investors should pay close attention to. This compression relates to new revenue being affected by costs associated with newly deployed infrastructure that hasn’t yet reached full utilization.

Think of it like buying equipment for a new business, but there’s no revenue yet. Naturally, the costs are there regardless. I think this perfectly describes what the industry calls a J-curve, where numbers look worse before they get better. For CoreWeave, the question is whether that upswing will happen. Given the financial backlog, which I will get into shortly, I think it will.

Management is also guiding for between $1.9 and $2 billion in revenue for the first quarter of 2026, and $12 to $13 billion for the entire year. In terms of spending, CapEx for 2026 is expected to be at least $30 billion, which is more than double what the company spent in 2025.

Why did CRWV stock drop 25% after earnings?

At the time of publication, the CoreWeave stock sits at about $73 per share, which is down roughly 25% since earnings despite market-beating top-line figures. But the earnings results weren’t the issue. What spooked the market was more than likely CoreWeave’s forward guidance, as they made it clear that the road to profitability is going to be longer than most investors expected- and more expensive at that.

Again, revenue guidance for the first quarter of 2026 is set to come in between $1.9 and $2 billion. At the mid-point, that lands at about $290 billion, which is below the Wall Street consensus of $2.29 billion. Moreover, management guided the first quarter adjusted operating income to come in between $0 and $40 million, which is practically breakeven.

Management also called the first quarter trough, meaning the bottom, in the margin context. CFO Nitin Agrawal’s exact remarks were:

We expect Q1 adjusted operating income between $0 and $40 million. Q1 represents the trough in our annual margin trajectory as we expect our CapEx deployments to be $6 billion to $7 billion of infrastructure in the quarter as we continue to bring online significant further capacity beyond the approximately 260 megawatts we added in Q4.

Personally, I think the market overreacted a bit, as it does, since CoreWeave isn’t dealing with a demand problem. What’s happening is a timing mismatch between when capital gets deployed and when revenue from that capital gets recognized. Specifically, data centers being built in the first quarter have a real cost associated with them, but there’s no revenue associated with them- at least not yet.

Management tagging the first quarter as a low point means the sequential ramp through the rest of 2026 should be steep, and the contracted backlog provides the foundation for that ramp- which I will discuss in the next section.

CoreWeave’s $66.8 billion backlog serves as the potential growth driver

If there’s one figure in this earnings release that investors should anchor to, I believe it’s the $66.8 billion in contracted revenue. This backlog also increased ~$11 billion sequentially from the previous quarter, and more than $50 billion compared to the same period last year. This is contractually locked-in customer spending that hasn’t been recognized as revenue.

In the report, management stated that the vast majority of planned deployments are directly supporting this long-dated contracted demand, where CoreWeave has direct visibility into long-term margins and cash flow. Intrator’s exact words were:

Our revenue backlog has grown to $66.8 billion, and the vast majority of our intended capital deployment is to directly support this long-dated contracted demand, where we have direct visibility into our long-term margins, underpinned by durable cash flow.

Simply put, this means CoreWeave knows what it will earn on these contracts and over what timeframe. Moreover, these new capacity contracts are expected to start generating sales by year-end, so the $30 billion spending number isn’t a shot in the dark. Its infrastructure is being built to support contracted customer commitments.

The number of customers also backs it up. Specifically, CoreWeave increased the number of customers committed to spending at least $1 million by nearly 150% during 2025, and added roughly twice as many new reserved instance customers in the fourth quarter than they had in any prior quarter. In case you’re unfamiliar, reserved instances are basically long-term capacity reservations where customers sign for a set amount of compute over a fixed period, and typically get a discount for that commitment.

There are also notable improvements in customer diversification. From hyperscalers, CoreWeave was able to expand to enterprise customers like CrowdStrike (CRWD), Midjourney, MercadoLibre (MELI), and more. That’s all critical since customer concentration, or specifically, hyperscaler concentration, has always been a present risk for cloud providers like CoreWeave.

If the backlog materializes as planned, it would represent roughly 13x of what CoreWeave produced in 2025, which is around $5.13 billion. Against 2026 forward guidance of up to $13 billion, it would still represent over 5x projected annual revenue in contracted work. Either way you look at it, the backlog towers over both current and near-term output.

Now, assuming that $66.8 billion is spread across an average contract life of roughly 4 to 5 years, which I’m basing on management's commentary around long-dated reserved contract agreements, that implies a potential ARR somewhere in the range of $13 to $17 billion, or about 3x what CoreWeave generated in 2025.

And CoreWeave is already guiding $12 to $13 billion for 2026. So the company has already locked in enough contracted demand to cover its own aggressive growth targets for the next several years, and that is before signing a single new deal. So at this point, the revenue is not hypothetical, but is contracted, waiting to be recognized.

I think that’s the disconnect. The market is pricing CoreWeave like the growth is uncertain, when in reality, a large portion of it is already spoken for.

Moreover, management referenced stronger targets for 2027 during the earnings calls. As Agrawal mentioned:

As we continue on our hyper-growth trajectory, we expect to exit 2026 with annualized run rate revenue of $17 billion to $19 billion, which we expect to grow to more than $30 billion of annualized run rate revenue as we exit 2027.

To me, this revenue backlog alone is the single most important de-risking factor and CoreWeave’s path to profitability. And right now, it seems like the market is not giving it enough credit.

Now, investments aren’t without risk, and with CoreWeave, the most immediate one is hard to miss.

Debt-fueled expansion and exploding interest expense might be a risk for CoreWeave

CoreWeave’s debt picture is a difficult one to gloss over, as it is both the company’s greatest enabler and, arguably, an existential threat. For full-year 2025, interest expense alone rose 240% to around $1.2 billion, while fourth-quarter interest expense rose 160% to $388 million. This expense is also one of the main reasons CoreWeave’s net loss is widening despite nearly tripling its revenue.

In the fourth quarter, CoreWeave generated $898 million in adjusted EBITDA but paid $388 million in interest, which means interest alone took over 43% of the adjusted EBITDA in the quarter, while about 40% for the entire year. For many, those ratios aren’t comfortable, and it’s likely to get worse before it gets better. Total debt stood at $21.37 billion at year-end, which is up over 170% year-over-year. Remember, CoreWeave plans to spend at least $30 billion in 2026, which means it may need to fund it from additional borrowing.

So, what’s the risk? Well, Intrator said in an interview that the company was willing to accept short-term margin compression as capacity is added to meet customer demand. You can read more about it here. That said, if the $66.8 billion backlog converts on schedule at a contracted margin, interest expenses would more than likely become manageable- once revenue catches up at scale.

And there’s another structural moat I think worth mentioning. CoreWeave has no near-term debt maturities before 2029, as mentioned in the fourth-quarter earnings calls. The only debt maturing before 2029 is self-amortizing contract-backed and vendor financing debt, which pays down gradually as contracted revenue flows in. The larger obligations do not come due until 2029 or later, so CoreWeave has a meaningful runway. This also eliminates near-term financing risk- which is a big deal when the direction of interest rates remains uncertain.

But of course, the absence of looming maturities doesn’t shrink the growing interest bill. It just means CoreWeave won’t experience a sudden cash crunch as it converts backlogs into revenue. To me, the mountain of debt is manageable, but it requires the backlog to play out.

What’s my verdict on CoreWeave?

Apparently, the conversation around CoreWeave keeps circling back to the debt load and margin compression. Fair concerns, indeed. But what rarely gets covered is the mismatch in how the market views CoreWeave’s capital cycle vs. how that cycle works.
