You see, infrastructure companies often borrow, build infrastructure, and then hope that demand shows up down the line. CoreWeave, however, borrows money after demand is locked in. Also, the company uses delayed draw term loans- which means borrowing doesn’t activate until the specific infrastructure tied to a specific customer comes online. I think that’s a fundamentally different approach from speculative capacity buildouts that burned telecom and early cloud providers in the past.

That said, investors should also be watching the pace at which contracted capacity begins generating revenue. If there’s a two to three quarter lag between deployment and revenue generation, the income statement will continue to look unpleasant even while the underlying business performs exactly as planned. That would be a kind of disconnect that creates opportunities for long-term investors who can see past the quarterly headlines.

With that, and everything outlined above, my Buy rating for CoreWeave remains in place.

data.title: WESCO: Strong Fundamentals At A Reasonable Price
data.description:
Investment thesis

Despite its prior solid performance, WESCO (WCC) currently trades at attractive multiples, which presents upside through the following durable components: structurally embedded data center exposure (2025 sales $4.3B, +50% YOY, approximately 18% of revenue) across sectors and strong ability to pay down debt (2026 FCF $500-$800M enabling debt reduction); and robust backlog visibility (+19% overall year-over-year, with CSS nearly +40% and UBS +23%). These points support my 'Buy' thesis for WESCO's stock.

Competitive Scene

Scale and logistics are key advantages in WESCO'S cyclical and competitive market. In this environment, W.W. Grainger, Inc. (GWW) is the closest competitor due to its focus on industrial distribution and MRO solutions.

Unlike other peers such as Hubbell Incorporated (HUBB) and nVent Electric plc (NVT), WESCO carries a much broader product portfolio across electrical, safety, and technical supplies. Its distribution reach is difficult to replicate for competitors as it operates a broader global distribution network across more than 50 countries.

However, WESCO depends heavily on third party suppliers for its product inventory. Minimal changes or disruptions can directly impact margins. Manufacturers like nVent and Hubbell have more control over the supply chain, giving them an advantage when conditions tighten.

By bringing together electrical, communication, and security systems, WESCO International gives customers in the large-scale construction/improvement of data centers a complete range of integrated products and services that help alleviate project complexity, increase supply chain resiliency, and provide support throughout the different stages of the data center's life cycle. Using this integrated approach, WESCO provides customers with an assortment of products ranging from power distribution to network connection solutions, to security systems, to value-added services including resource management, partnerships, and coordination of vendors, all of which reduce the need to use multiple suppliers and have the potential to minimize delays and compatibility issues.

The following table compares profitability metrics for WESCO and its peers. The companies shared in the table have similar margins; however, WESCO shows lower gross profit and net income margins due to high SG&A expenses that directly affect profitability. WESCO presents stronger revenue growth than competitors such as Grainger and Hubbell due to increased project activity, although it does not compare with nVent growth.

Strategic Direction

According to the comments from the CSS segment, records for Data Center sales reach $4.3 billion with a year-over-year growth of over fifty percent. In addition, acquisitions related to Rahi Systems, entroCIM, and Ascent implied further evidence of this trend. At the same time, overall demand for the EES and UBS segments is currently weak due to continuing cyclicality; however, continued investments in digital transformation initiatives to promote efficiency and operating leverage offset concerns from the underlying market conditions due to increased product diversity.

Scale and depth of supplier relationships are the company's two largest competitive advantages. This gives the company a competitive edge over competitors like Grainger who provide electrical products while providing broader end to end solutions than either Hubbell or nVent provide. Despite this competitive advantage, if the company experiences supplier disruptions, such disruption could put pressure on the company's margins and not allow digital innovations to provide improved supply chain resiliency.

Strong projected free cash flow of $500-$800 million will support debt reduction. Shareholders should benefit from strong free cash flow through dividend increases and share repurchases, in addition to reducing the impact of share issuance due to dilution. Recently completed refinancing activities (senior note issuance to pay off higher interest debt) will further optimize the company's balance sheet and address elevated leverage risk.

Enduring secular trends in AI and digitization, driven by strong Hyperscale demand, are accelerating the growth of WESCO’s Data Centers. This is enabling the full lifecycle phased solution model: Power & Electrical Infrastructure (Grey Space, ~20% Data Center Sales via EES), Advanced Connectivity, IT Infrastructure, and AI/Compute Environments (White Space, ~80% CSS), along with Construction, Commissioning, Ongoing Operations, Modernization, and Managed Services; thus maximizing WESCO's capability to capture value per project while evolving with customer demand. WESCO will reach $4.3B in Data Center sales in 2025 (up ~50% YoY with $1.2B from 4Q, up ~30% YoY) while its CSS Backlog has increased nearly 40% to a record high. WESCO is well positioned to capture additional market share and continue this momentum into 2026 and beyond, as a more durable avenue for Demand than shorter-cycle Construction or Industrial markets.

Industry Landscape

According to a Business Research Company report, the machinery, equipment, and supplies wholesalers market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 6.4% from 2026 to 2030. As depicted in the chart below, total nonresidential construction spending in the U.S. has recently remained flat after a period of strong momentum, which suggests industry demand has stabilized. Total nonresidential construction spending in the U.S. is a key demand driver, as the company supplies electrical components used in industrial projects.

Consolidated Q4 Results

Net sales for the quarter increased 10.3%, driven by volume growth across all three segments the company has and favorable pricing. As shown below, the end of Q4 2025 represents a significant increase in backlog caused by strong demand (19% year-over-year).

Gross margin remained flat sequentially from the previous year quarter. There was an overall decrease in gross margin compared to last year due to an overall decrease in gross margin across the three segment.

Higher salaries, increased cost to operate facilities and an increase in transportation costs drove an 11.4% rise in adjusted SG&A.

The total impact of the Series A Preferred Stock redemption on earnings per diluted share resulted in an increase of 10.12% in adjusted earnings.

The quarterly results highlight that there was a sound level of disciplined sales growth, along with some severe cost pressures that have negatively impacted profitability levels. In my opinion, the overall quarter continued to show growth that was both driven by net sales and adjusted EPS. I remain very optimistic about the future of this company's growth opportunity despite the fact that SG&A expenses may put further pressure on profitability in future quarters.

Segment Performance

Electrical & Electronic Solutions (EES)

Organic sales were up 8% year-over-year as a result of strong demand and a high volume of project activity. Adjusted EBITDA margins were improved by 50 basis points sequentially; however, year-over-year performance was negatively impacted by both project activity and product mix. Backlogs were up 6% year-over-year; the current growth of the company relates to organic sales; however, margin pressures from lower-margin project activities resulted in a decline in gross profit margins of 50 basis points year-over-year.

Communications & Security Solutions (CSS)

Year over year, organic sales are up 17%, with data centers being the main growth catalyst at 50%. Adjusted EBITDA margin increased by 90 bps on a sequential basis, driven by significant operating cost leverage due to double digit sales growth. Backlog grew nearly 40% year over year due to growth in data center projects. The data center project segment is a primary driver of both sales and backlog growth, leading to relatively stable operating cost leverage within the EBITDA margin.

Utility & Broadband Solutions (UBS)

Organic sales increased 3% year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA margin decreased 90 bps YOY and sequentially down 120 bps due to competitive pressures in the public power market. Backlog increased 23% YOY reflecting new project activity. Stable sales growth suggests there is limited current cost pressure, which is supported by a strong backlog.

Takeaway

The Electrical & Electronic Solutions segment reflects strong demand and improving margins due to project activity. The Communications & Security Solutions segment reflects strong demand, stable operating cost leverage and improving margins. The Utility & Broadband Solutions segment showed stable sales and increased backlog due to new project activity. With current segment performance, I believe WESCO is positioned for continued growth which may reflect in further quarters.

Financial Health

As shown in the chart below, net debt in the last 5 quarters has increased, indicating rising debt levels.

In the table below, the company remains highly leveraged as shown by its total debt/equity ratio of 134.53%. However, its current ratio reflects solid liquidity, suggesting strong short term liquidity. Its metrics remain in line with peers, suggesting that leverage is currently manageable relative to industry criteria.

Valuation

I calculated WACC assuming a cost of debt of 5.74%, a cost of equity of 10.12%, and a capital structure with 31.44% debt and 68.56% equity. The pre-tax cost of debt was calculated by dividing the interest expense by the total debt. The cost of debt after tax was calculated using this formula: Cost of Debt*(1-Effective Tax Rate).

I calculated the cost of equity using the following formula: (Risk Free Rate)+Beta*(Market Return-Risk Free Rate). The risk-free rate is the yield of the 10Y U.S. Treasury Bonds, and the market return is an estimated average of the S&P500 Index (SP500) over the years. The capital structure breakdown uses the company's market capitalization as equity and total debt as debt, as depicted in the WACC table below.

Furthermore, the growth rate used to calculate the FFCF was the rate the machinery, equipment and supplies wholesalers is expected to grow annually. WESCO's historical performance positions it to mirror industry growth. The perpetual growth rate is set to replicate the long-term GDP performance of the U.S. economy to project that the company will keep growing at a moderate rate.

The formula I used to calculate equity value was adding the sum of FFCF and the cash on hand and then subtracting it from the total debt. Finally, the DCF price per share was calculated by dividing the equity value by the current shares outstanding.

The intrinsic value derived by my DCF analysis is approximately $289 in the base case. In addition to my base DCF model, I included several scenarios to account for the industry cyclicality. The range I obtained is $232-$338 per share by varying FFCF growth rates. In my view, the optimistic case is the most suitable for my investment thesis, as accelerating backlog, project expansion and visible operating leverage justify the growth rates for my optimistic case of $338 per share.

As can be seen in the peer table below, WESCO P/E GAAP TTM and FWD ratio sit at the lowest range among its peers. This demonstrates that its valuation is relatively low, supporting my investment thesis. Current valuation justifies low margins which imply operating leverage from project expansion.

Risks

While WESCO has strong liquidity as reflected by its current ratio, the company has high leverage with rising debt levels. With a total debt/equity ratio of 134.53%, it suggests that if margins tighten or projects slow, this could increase balance sheet risk.

Industry trends can directly impact the company's operations. Total nonresidential construction spending growth has slowed after a period of strong growth; if this trend persists, it will negatively impact WESCO project demand.

Additionally, supplier disruptions remain a risk to margins and operating leverage, although digital transformation initiatives are intended to strengthen supply chain resiliency.

Conclusion

To sum up, WESCO's strong investment thesis is backed by its exposure to data centers that are structurally embedded, its developing backlog that is currently accelerating, and the substantial amount of free cash flow it generates, all of which allow the Company to continue its deleveraging. The company's life cycle solutions model and scale advantages allow it to take a larger portion of the long-term growth in infrastructure, while improving its operating leverage over time. Margin pressure and cyclicality are risks, but the current valuation multiple for WESCO represents a good entry point relative to the durability of these growth drivers and therefore warrants a Buy rating on the stock.

Market Overview

Global markets in the fourth quarter were shaped by the interplay of central bank policy, shifting rate expectations, and recurring geopolitical and trade-related headlines. The Federal Reserve lowered rates at its final meeting of the year, but investors continued to debate how quickly additional easing might unfold as growth signals stayed resilient and inflation readings were interpreted through the lens of data disruptions tied to the government shutdown. Equity markets advanced in a more selective fashion, with ongoing enthusiasm around AI-related investment alongside periodic rotations that favored smaller-cap and value segments at times versus the most crowded mega-cap areas. Outside the U.S., a weaker dollar and relatively more attractive valuations supported international equities; within that, emerging markets also posted gains but tended to trail developed markets modestly, with dispersion across countries and style factors remaining an important driver of results. Meanwhile, intermittent tariff developments and geopolitical risks—including renewed tensions in the Middle East and Venezuela—contributed to bouts of volatility that influenced energy pricing and reinforced demand for perceived safe havens such as gold.

Performance Summary

  • Hartford Moderate Allocation Fund (I share)(HBAIX) underperformed its benchmark during the period.
  • The main driver of the fund’s underperformance versus the benchmark was performance of the underlying positions relative to their individual benchmarks. In particular, Hartford World Bond Fund, Hartford Core Equity Fund and Hartford Strategic Income ETF underperformed their respective benchmarks.
  • Asset allocation decisions did not materially impact performance during the period.

Positioning & Outlook

  • Our stance remains overweight equities and underweight fixed income, with balanced regional and style exposure. We expect lower US interest rates and increased government spending in Europe, Japan, and China to support equity markets and economic growth, while tight spreads* limit upside across fixed income markets.
  • We expect steadier markets as rates move lower and fiscal support continues, but current optimism limits room for disappointment. We prefer cyclical and quality growth companies and maintain a slight large-company bias.

*Spreads are the difference in yields between two fixed-income securities with the same maturity but originating from different investment sectors.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway's Handoff Year: Buffett's Final Lessons, Abel's First Blueprint
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) just held their annual meeting to discuss their 2025 results and officially pass the torch from Warren Buffett to Greg Able. Berkshires results were solid but not spectacular but that’s not the real story. The biggest development is the structural as Berkshire’s entering 2026 with formal CEO transition that will control a balance sheet with $369.15 billion in on-hand liquidity between cash and short-term T-bills in addition to a stock portfolio valued at $297.78 billion. After reading Greg Abel’s letter to shareholders it reads more like an operating constitution rather than a folklore approach that I feel Mr. Buffett channeled. Warren Buffett is still the chairman but Greg Abel officially has the reins. The combination of succession clarity and the amount of dry powder on hand could signal long-term opportunities for shareholders as Mr. Abel looks to make his mark on this historic company.

I’m not ready to upgrade my investment thesis just yet as I want to see how the first couple of quarters unfold as Mr. Able seems to be about systematizing Buffett’s discipline rather than reinventing it. The decentralized model Berkshire took has led to a conservative balance sheet and a value driven capital allocation framework. I feel as if Berkshire often is looked at as an insurance float and portfolio story which is incomplete due to the underlying businesses. Their edge has been having the ability to stay liquid which allows them to be forced into decisions and having the option to deploy capital when others can’t. I believe Berkshire will continue their intrinsic value at a low to mid single-digit pace but I am not sure that will be enough to expand its multiple. The questions I want answered is whether or not Mr. Able will acquire additional companies, add to their equity positions or start to repurchase shares.

Following up on my previous article about Berkshire Hathaway

Back in December I had downgraded my investment thesis on Berkshire to neutral from bullish (can be read here) Since then shares of Berkshire had underperformed the S&P 500 as its declined by -3.06% compared to -1.01%. I had discussed how Berkshires portfolio was heavily weighted toward cash, Treasuries, and non-tech operating businesses which would limit upside appreciation if interest rates fell. As the risk-free rate of return declines Berkshire won’t be generating as much interest from their allocation into T-bills. In addition to my concern about less interest income being generated I wanted to see if there would be a strategic shift under Mr. Able. After reading through the annual report I am still neutral on shares of Berkshire and I will remain on the sidelines for now as I want to see how Mr. Abel leads Berkshire over the next couple of quarters.

There is a shift in tone from Warren Buffet to Greg Able in the annual letters

In my interpretation of the 2023 letter Mr. Buffett speaks like a permanent owner and leans into simple examples that seem sticky. He highlighted Berkshire’s long-duration positions in Coca-Cola (KO) and American Express (AXP) while having a jokingly tone about Berkshire not being big on newcomers. Mr. Buffett even made the reference as their multi-decade holding period as a Rip Van Winkle slumber. The deeper message is their capital allocation discipline as Mr. Buffett continued to remind shareholders that share buybacks would remain price dependent. His feeling continued to be that buying back shares at a discount to buisness value is sensible but buying them back when the buisness value is at a premium not in Berkshire’s best interest.

In the 2024 letter Mr. Buffett toned down the folklore aspect and leaned a bit into a governing message. He defied his tradition of communicating as if shareholders were the CEO and Buffett’s family were passive investors trusting them with their savings. He formalized several things and dedicated a section to mistakes as he discussed errors in capital allocation, people decisions, and warning against delaying correction. This was the letter where Mr. Buffett made the succession plan a reality by discussing that at the age of 94 it won’t be long before he is replaced by Greg Able as CEO. Mr. Buffett also reinforced Berkshires model of controlling operating businesses on one side and partial ownership of public companies on the other. Mr. Buffett emphasized operating earnings rather than volatility in the GAAP numbers while highlighting their $47.4 billion in operating earnings as he cited EBITDA being a flawed metric once again.

Mr. Abels first letter in the 2025 annual report read to me like an operating system. He framed Berkshires culture as a system for generating long-term performance and cited Charlie Munger’s comments about him retaining the culture. Mr. Abel promised that the decentralized model wouldn’t change as there would be autonomy paired with accountability. He explicitly warned against focusing on short-term earnings as he is trying to preserve the Berkshire machine that Mr. Buffett built rather than trying to be Mr. Buffett. Mr. Able also discussed how he is elevating risk management as a CEO level duty and indicated that the CEO is responsible for serving as Chief Risk Officer.

My feeling is that there is a dramatic shift in tone between leadership styles. Mr. Buffett’s letters have history read as why Berkshire is doing things where as Mr. Abel’s first letter read as how they will be doing things. The business commentary that I read indicated that he is already framing Berkshire’s largest non-insurance assets as multi-year improvement stories, not static cash cows. He also emphasized operating margin as the key KPI and stated that it improved to 34.5% in 2025 from 32.0% in 2024. He seems to be telegraphing operational focus with measurable accountability as he signaled that he will be disappointed without substantial improvement over the next few years as 1% of improvement generates about $230 million of incremental operating cash flow. His early signals can be taken as bullish for Berkshires identity as the future capital allocation is likely to remain price disciplined but that could also become a double edged sword causing Berkshire to miss out on opportunities as the GDP and economy grows.

Why I am worried Mr. Abel will be cautious at a time when revenue growth is critical

The United States 3-Month Bill Yield (US3M) has fallen -1.82% since September 2023 when it was yielding 5.50% and now it’s producing 3.68%. CME Group is projecting that there will be at least 2 rate cuts on 2026 with more than a 25% chance 3 or more occur. Goldman Sachs (GS) head of investment grade credit Jonny Fine believes the Fed will cut 4 times in 2026 with the first cut coming in June. This is not bullish for Berkshire as they have $744.42 billion sitting on the balance sheet in investments and equities. In 2024 Berkshire generated $21.83 billion in interest and dividends which increased to $23.26 billion in 2025. This is an increase of 6.58% ($1.44 billion) YoY. While Berkshire is generating more in interest and dividend income on an annual basis than some companies generate in top-line revenue there is a fundamental problem as rates decline.

The total amount of investments from T-bills to equities including cash on hand has grown 10.55% ($71.07 billion) YoY to $744.42 billion. The growth in invested assets is outpacing the amount of interested being generated as it grew $1.44 billion (6.58%) YoY. The total amount of income generated from the interest being paid on its cash, T-Bills, fixed maturity assets, loans and financial receivables and equity method investments in addition to the divided income from the equity portfolio has a total yield of 3.12% which is 0.12% lower than in 2024. As rates continue to decline Berkshire is at risk of generating less income on a percentage basis. If Berkshire allocates more capital to companies in the equity portfolio that either don’t pay a dividend or pay less than 3.12% then the amount of interest and dividend income generated in 2026 will have a significantly lower yield.

United States 3-Month Bill Yield (US3M)

Berkshire’s wholly owned businesses provide ballast, but ballast doesn’t usually win a sprint. The market doesn’t pay premium multiples for stability alone when the incremental return on the balance sheet is sliding. As the risk-free rate of return declines Berkshires pool of liquidity could actually start behaving like a drag on headline growth. The core issue isn’t whether Berkshire remains a great business it’s whether the rate of change in earnings and cash generation can keep up with an S&P 500 that is increasingly dominated by companies compounding revenue at double-digit rates. This is why I am focused on how Mr. Abel will deploy capital. As the income from the invested capital is likely to decline the burden shifts to operating earnings growth and reinvestment returns.

This means that Berkshire will need to accelerate reinvestment into wholly owned businesses at high incremental returns, find acquisitions that can move the needle, or buy back shares aggressively if and when it trades below intrinsic value. The problem is that the first two options require a willingness to lean in when uncertainty is high and the third option would require Mr. Abel to think about buybacks as a sort of acquisition if Berkshire doesn’t trade below its intrinsic value. Mr. Buffett has historically been patient but he’s also been decisive when prices and probabilities were attractive. My worry is that Mr. Abel’s version of patience looks more like caution which could lead to preserving optionality, waiting for perfect pitches, and keeping the fortress balance sheet even more fortified than it already is.

Risks to investing in Berkshire Hathaway

While Berkshire is a unicorn that would be next to impossible to replicate there are significant risk factors to consider before allocating capital to it. Berkshire may not be able to compound at the previous rate that investors are used to and the biggest risks are a transition in leadership and capital allocation. There is no guarantee that Mr. Able can go from the shadows to the spotlight. The reality is that even if the operating businesses remain strong the market may apply a lower valuation multiple if Mr. Able shifts away from Berkshires historical tendencies. Berkshires also faces business and macro risk across insurance and regulated assets. It’s insurance companies carry tail risk from catastrophe years, reserve adjustments, and pricing cycle swings while its investment income is likely to decline in a falling-rate environment. As rates decline we could find ourselves in an environment here the market is rewarding double-digit growth which is the type of market Berkshire would likely underperform in. Investors should do their own due diligence before making any investment decisions.

Conclusion

I believe that Berkshire will trade as a company in transition as we go from the Buffett narrative to Mr. Ables framework. Buffett has had a common theme of buying and investing in wonderful businesses, being patient and having discipline when it comes to buybacks. Mr. Abel’s first letter codifies the Berkshire system capital discipline, and CEO-level risk management. I don’t believe that there is enough data for me to turn bullish on Berkshire yet. I am worried that Mr. Able may be to cautious in a lower rate environment where it’s fortress balance sheet can’t work as hard as it once did for Berkshire. I think this is a wait and see game for the next quarter or two to see how Mr. Able reacts to being at the helm and what types of decisions he will be making. The market didn’t seem inspired after Berkshires earnings and this is not the perception investors want the market to have of Berkshires new CEO.

data.title: United Airlines' 2026 Just Got Much More Cloudy
data.description:
The latest United States/Israel military campaign targeting Iran is already having a profound impact on the global travel industry, which almost always is disproportionately impacted by macroeconomic and geopolitical events. While the conflict is playing out on the other side of the world from the United States, U.S. airlines are being impacted by second- and third-order impacts. Although I will be looking at the economic impacts for the larger airline industry, I will focus on United Airlines (UAL), which faces a combination of potentially negative impacts that could impact UAL more than other U.S. airlines.

U.S. Airlines Will Be Indirectly Impacted by the Iran Conflict

The impact from the Iran conflict is not equal to all countries or even to specific airlines. Because of longstanding sanctions, no Iranian airline participates in a meaningful way in global air transportation. In contrast, a number of Middle Eastern airlines operate from hubs that are located less than 200 miles from Iran. Several of those airlines operate at hubs, including Dubai, Doha, and Abu Dhabi, which have grown into major global airline hubs. Dubai rivals Atlanta, the world’s busiest airport by passengers handled, in airline seats offered. While Israel is hundreds of miles away from Iran, its airspace and airline operations have been repeatedly interrupted because of war directly with Iran or with its proxies in the region, but that has not been the case with most of the rest of the Middle East. Many airline hubs, including Dubai, have near-complete suspension of air service for more than four days as of the date of writing of this article. Most of the airlines in the Middle East are either government-owned or privately held.

European airlines are generally well-connected with the Middle East, as are airlines in South Asia; Middle East airline hubs serve as important connectors between South Asia and the rest of the world. Most European and Indian airlines are publicly held, some with stock that is traded on U.S. exchanges. Indian airlines have seen a greater impact on their operations because of the conflict than European airlines.

The “outer ring” of impact to the airline industry is to airlines in other parts of the world, including the Americas and the United States. Before the most recent military action against Iran began, American (AAL), Delta (DAL), and United (UAL) combined operated less than 10 flights/day to just three cities in the Middle East – Tel Aviv, Dubai, and Doha.

Thus, it is second- and third-order impacts that have the potential to most negatively impact U.S. and European airlines.

Fuel Price Hikes Could Cut Airline Earnings

It seems obvious to state that any expansion of military activity outside the Middle East would significantly increase the risk for the global airline industry. At this point, there is no assumption that will occur.

There are a number of other impacts that are impacting the global airline industry, including U.S. airlines, and I believe it is the combination of these risks that is particularly worrisome for several airlines, including United Airlines. The most significant impact is on the cost of fuel, the second largest expense for most Western airlines behind labor costs. During the past week, crude oil prices have risen 14 percent. The impact on refined products is not yet clear, but it is expected that jet fuel prices will increase as much or more than the increase in crude oil. Most U.S. airlines do not hedge crude oil; the big three U.S. global carriers largely ended their hedging programs in the decade after 9/11, while Southwest (LUV) stopped hedging as part of its restructuring as a result of Elliott Investment’s involvement with the company. UAL pays jet fuel costs at the high end of the U.S. airline average because of its presence in California and Northeast U.S. markets. At the opposite end, Delta typically pays a discount to industry fuel costs because of its refinery ownership; that benefit increases proportionately as fuel becomes more expensive and when refining imbalances exist between production of jet fuel and other refined products. While the current conflict with Iran does not show any signs of impacting the balance of refined products, refinery capacity is being negatively impacted due to refinery and pipeline closures, which are negatively impacting fuel costs on the west coast; United is the largest civilian consumer of jet fuel on the west coast as a result of its large transpacific hub at San Francisco.

High fuel costs have often been the catalyst that has pushed weak airlines into bankruptcy reorganization or eradicated expected profits. While UAL was projecting mid- to high-single-digit net margins for 2026, other airlines are more fragile. Spirit Airlines (FLYYQ) is operating in Chapter 11 bankruptcy and had expected to emerge this spring. American, JetBlue (JBLU), and Southwest are all in the midst of their own strategic restructurings that they believe will raise their profitability for 2026; lower profitability could lead to reduced capacity and support higher fares or at least offset demand reduction. Fuel costs for airlines have been in decline for the past three years, which has increased profitability as well as allowed some airlines to invest in their product and in higher employee compensation.

This Could Be a Bad Time for Capacity Growth

Capacity addition remains a concern for U.S. airlines in 2026. In 2025, demand weakness in the first half of the year led to capacity restraint in the second half of the year, including capacity reductions by some of the financially weaker airlines. The second quarter of 2026 indicates that several airlines will be growing capacity at 5% or more, levels far in excess of GDP growth, which analysts have seen as being problematic for yields. United’s second quarter 2026 capacity growth is expected to be slightly less than American’s 6+% and much of that capacity does not include the massive capacity growth both carriers are adding at Chicago O’Hare where the two are vying for airport gates through increased usage. The FAA just announced last week that it is stepping in to impose schedule caps at the airport to prevent operational problems that it believes will occur because the airport is scheduled beyond its capacity. Airlines have to make capacity decisions months in advance, which means that government-required reductions will lead to capacity that can’t be successfully deployed elsewhere in such a short period of time.

United has led the U.S. airline industry in absolute capacity growth since the covid pandemic. UAL has stated that it believed that the international demand would allow UAL to gain more market share, and in that respect, they have been right. UAL is already the world’s largest airline based on capacity – available seat miles, or ASMs, an airline industry-specific metric and operates the largest international operation among U.S. airlines. Based on history with other geopolitical events, the domestic market will hold up better than international markets.

Heavy Capex Could Be Problematic

To support its aggressive growth, UAL has the largest aircraft order book in the U.S. airline industry and also far larger than the entire U.S. airline industry combined. UAL has commitments for over $57 billion in new aircraft over the next seven years and beyond; in 2026, UAL is expected to spend more than $12 billion on capex for 124 new aircraft, or more than 10% of the number of aircraft currently in its fleet. In contrast, DAL has the second largest order book with a value of almost $16 billion, with $3.65 billion to be delivered in 2026; DAL’s new aircraft deliveries represent less than 5% of its current mainline fleet size. However, DAL has announced orders from both Airbus and Boeing and converted options earlier this year, so DAL’s fleet spend for 2028 and beyond will increase beyond its fleet commitments in its 10-K. UAL’s very large capex for 2026 would likely have exceeded its cash generation even in the best environment but could become problematic should the industry encounter sustained headwinds.

Other Global Regions Are Demand-Challenged

The timing of this military initiative could be problematic for the airline industry. Not only has the second quarter become the prime earnings period for U.S. airlines as schools increasingly shift their vacations earlier in May, with schools in many parts of the country back in session in August. Spring Break season is currently in process, and airlines generally report strong bookings; those types of trips generally are to sun destinations within the U.S. and in the Caribbean and Latin America. Recent violence in Mexico is reportedly impacting travel, although no official data has been released at this point. In addition, Canadian visits to the U.S. continue to be down esp. to Las Vegas.

UAL’s Labor Costs Are Certain to Rise

Finally, United faces a particularly unique challenge in that it has open labor contracts with a number of employee groups, including its flight attendants, who have not received raises in more than five years. The company is more publicly vocal that it believes an agreement can be reached and says that its margin guidance includes expected increases in labor costs, but it is impossible to know if its labor groups will settle for the amount that UAL is expecting. All the other large airlines have predictable and settled labor costs other than Alaska (ALK), which is in the merger integration process as a result of its acquisition of Hawaiian Airlines.

An Early Containment of the Situation Is Needed But Far From Certain

There were positive developments in the conflict on Tuesday. The U.S. and Israel said they have significantly degraded Iran’s offensive and defensive military capabilities, which is evidenced by lower numbers of missiles landing in other countries of the Middle East. However, Iran still has a significant number of drones, which are expected to continue to threaten the region. The price of crude oil closed down from its intraday highs on Tuesday in part due to the U.S. government’s commitment to provide commercial maritime war insurance in key areas of the Middle East. Multiple countries, including the U.S., are assisting in the evacuation of their citizens from the Middle East. Governments, including the U.A.E., are redesigning air corridors to allow airline operations to return in greater numbers. Some governments are providing military jet escorts of civilian airliners through the region. Cancellation rates at some Middle East air hubs have begun to decline.

Still, guidance from the U.S. indicates that the war efforts could continue for days or weeks. Global oil supplies are not verifiably reliable, so prices will not likely come back down. Traveler sentiment regarding international travel is not known at this point. A general macroeconomic softening could cause some consumers to delay or cancel expenditures including travel, esp. for those that are funding their retirement through strong stock market performance.

The airline industry has long been impacted sooner and more strongly than other segments of the economy, and that is likely to be the case again this year, which was shaping up to be strong for much of the world’s airlines, including United Airlines. While UAL has made enormous strides over the past five years in growing its profitability, it is moving increasingly into higher risk strategies. While there are factors that could offset some of the risk, including UAL’s position relative to low-cost and ultra-low-cost carriers that have struggled, other airlines have lower risk profiles for other elements.

As we enter the final month of the first quarter, airlines would have been expected to update their guidance for the first quarter, although that is not likely to come until there is more stability regarding the war. Winter weather was particularly harsh in several areas of the U.S., with larger than usual numbers of flight cancellations.

The current level of uncertainty regarding the airline industry merits an overall cautious approach to the industry. Indications of significant impact in the medium to long term in any of the areas could significantly erode investor confidence in UAL; at present, I believe it is prudent to downgrade UAL to hold.
